{"action":"closed","number":7268,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/pulls/7268","id":2071218279,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCJ0aI857dEhn","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268","diff_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/7268","number":7268,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Speedup triangulation of edges","user":{"login":"Czaki","id":3826210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM4MjYyMTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3826210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki","html_url":"https://github.com/Czaki","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Czaki/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"# References and relevant issues # Description Similar to #7256 this PR is a speedup of face triangulation by avoiding a lot of reallocation of arrays. After long investigation, I found that using best way to speed this up is by avoiding the allocation of additional arrays by using numba. For shapes that do not have bevels: On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6775d94-a88b-4a99-959f-f4b6f7f768aa) In this PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a3a302c-2eb1-4ecd-8c38-a07617512ede) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d7f00a8-4d55-4bb2-9f49-955bc746e01f) As you may see, the time of `triangulate_edge` is reduced from 44s to 17s. Half of it is numba compilation. With sharp edges that require bevel join, the speedup is even bigger: Main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2a55f13-6ba7-4f1d-9ff9-56fa46fdf49c) PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6279350e-1e50-441e-a538-4f01b5420d40) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3c992928-b63c-42d7-be6d-d0ec7ec620f5) This PR also fixes the bevel join On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a24bc31-9777-4d60-81d5-ff6cc1b0d1a2) With PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b167a4d5-9bce-442c-80c6-d5a0c90e5f8d) In this PR I have added `examples/dev/triangle_edge.py` with a set of helper functions to visualize triangulation. These are helpful for debugging of triangulation problems. # Algorithm explanation Here I add some explanation to the code implementation of this PR. ## Calculation of miter vector # This is a screenshot of miter join: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08d2dd78-bb02-4da8-baa5-7b0596e1c3c7) And let's assume markings as on image: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0cec9ff-3786-4edc-805a-d45e58f33aab) The $\vec{BA}$ (blue) is half of the normal vector from vertex $n$ to $n+1$. The $\vec{AC}$ is minus half of the normal vector from vertex $n-1$ to $n$. The vector $\vec{AD}$ (green) is orthogonal to line $BA$ of length $0.5$ Based on these vectors, we could easily calculate $\vec{BC} = \vec{BA} + \vec{AC}$. However, the length of $|GC|$ may be different from 1. We would like to get the vector $\vec{BE}$ We will use here trigonometry and [Intercept theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercept_theorem) We know that $\frac{|GC|}{|AC|} = \sin{\alpha}$ and $|AC| = \frac{1}{2}$ so $|GC| = \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{2}$ From intercept theorem, we know that $\frac{|BC|}{|GC|} = \frac{|BE|}{|FE|}$. The length of $|FE| = \frac{1}{2}$. So $|BE| = |BC| \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$. That mens that $\vec{BE} = \vec{BC} \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ ## Bevel limit # In general, we use miter join, however, if the angle is sharp, it may result in strange artifacts (see #6706). So if the join will be too long (The length of $\vec{BA}$ vector from above), we would like to change to a bevel join. So we need to calculate the length of $\vec{BE}$. We would like to avoid calculation of square root in every turn of the loop. From Pythagoras theorem we know that $|BE|^2 = |EF|^2 + |BF|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + |BF|^2$ We also know that $|GA| = \frac{\cos{\alpha}}{2}$ so $|BG| = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2}$. From miter vector calculation and intercept theorem we know that $|BF| = \frac{|BG|}{\sin{\alpha}} = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}$ So we know the length of the vector $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + \left(\frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2{\alpha} + 1^2 - 2\cos{\alpha} + \cos^2{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} = \frac{2 - 2 \cos{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} $$ Using trigonometric identity $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{1 - \cos^2{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{(1 - \cos{\alpha})(1 + \cos{\alpha})} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}}$$ So if we would like to check condition $|BE| > limit$ we could use the following equation: $$ \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}} > {limit}^2$$ So: $$ \frac{1}{2 \cdot {limit}^2} - 1 > \cos{\alpha} $$ It means that if $\cos\alpha$ is below a given threshold, we should use bevel join. ## Too long vector in bevel join # As I showed above, this PR is fixing the bevel join by fixing the position of inner edge of the bevel join. You may see an example in this image (see the longest yellow vector): ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/58e13821-a1b6-4dd0-a49b-ade97edf9e73) The schematic of generating long edge: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/329b2b61-3c9d-4050-9623-7053c1f46248) As users may use shapes with different length edges, we cannot use a hard-coded limit for the length of the offset vector that creates the problems pointed on above image. However, when calculating the normal vectors, we need to calculate the edge width, so we may save them for later usage and limit the length of such problematic offset vector to length of shortest adjusted edge. In the Bevel limit section, we have an explicit formula for miter offset length. However, it contains a square root. And calculation of a square root is expensive on current CPU, so we would like to avoid it. However, the described problem happens only when $\measuredangle BAC$ is close to $180\degree$, but in such a situation, the $|\vec{BA} + \vec{AC}|$ is close to 1 (as resulted vector is close to diameter). So we could approximate the length of vector $\vec{BC}$ as 1. Then the estimated length of vector $\vec{BE}$ is $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ So if $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ is bigger than the length of half of any adjusted edges, we could calculate a new scaling factor with value $$\pm 0.5 \cdot\min(vec1\textunderscore{}len, vec2\textunderscore{}len))$$ # Additional notes ## Artifacts There are still artifacts in rendering {"repository_id":144513571,"push_id":21645493702,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"b2edccd6e40e04467ccfeec0257c2160783f7187","before":"fe19b7eb65b020d1cfcc4b62f183f835ae64a24a","commits":[{"sha":"b2edccd6e40e04467ccfeec0257c2160783f7187","author":{"email":"f2e539f865a022dd4c686415db2ca1deb56cad41@gmail.com","name":"Grzegorz Bokota"},"message":"Speedup triangulation of edges (#7268) # References and relevant issues # Description Similar to #7256 this PR is a speedup of face triangulation by avoiding a lot of reallocation of arrays. After long investigation, I found that using best way to speed this up is by avoiding the allocation of additional arrays by using numba. For shapes that do not have bevels: On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6775d94-a88b-4a99-959f-f4b6f7f768aa) In this PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a3a302c-2eb1-4ecd-8c38-a07617512ede) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d7f00a8-4d55-4bb2-9f49-955bc746e01f) As you may see, the time of `triangulate_edge` is reduced from 44s to 17s. Half of it is numba compilation. With sharp edges that require bevel join, the speedup is even bigger: Main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2a55f13-6ba7-4f1d-9ff9-56fa46fdf49c) PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6279350e-1e50-441e-a538-4f01b5420d40) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3c992928-b63c-42d7-be6d-d0ec7ec620f5) This PR also fixes the bevel join On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a24bc31-9777-4d60-81d5-ff6cc1b0d1a2) With PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b167a4d5-9bce-442c-80c6-d5a0c90e5f8d) In this PR I have added `examples/dev/triangle_edge.py` with a set of helper functions to visualize triangulation. These are helpful for debugging of triangulation problems. # Algorithm explanation Here I add some explanation to the code implementation of this PR. ## Calculation of miter vector # This is a screenshot of miter join: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08d2dd78-bb02-4da8-baa5-7b0596e1c3c7) And let's assume markings as on image: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0cec9ff-3786-4edc-805a-d45e58f33aab) The $\vec{BA}$ (blue) is half of the normal vector from vertex $n$ to $n+1$. The $\vec{AC}$ is minus half of the normal vector from vertex $n-1$ to $n$. The vector $\vec{AD}$ (green) is orthogonal to line $BA$ of length $0.5$ Based on these vectors, we could easily calculate $\vec{BC} = \vec{BA} + \vec{AC}$. However, the length of $|GC|$ may be different from 1. We would like to get the vector $\vec{BE}$ We will use here trigonometry and [Intercept theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercept_theorem) We know that $\frac{|GC|}{|AC|} = \sin{\alpha}$ and $|AC| = \frac{1}{2}$ so $|GC| = \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{2}$ From intercept theorem, we know that $\frac{|BC|}{|GC|} = \frac{|BE|}{|FE|}$. The length of $|FE| = \frac{1}{2}$. So $|BE| = |BC| \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$. That mens that $\vec{BE} = \vec{BC} \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ ## Bevel limit # In general, we use miter join, however, if the angle is sharp, it may result in strange artifacts (see #6706). So if the join will be too long (The length of $\vec{BA}$ vector from above), we would like to change to a bevel join. So we need to calculate the length of $\vec{BE}$. We would like to avoid calculation of square root in every turn of the loop. From Pythagoras theorem we know that $|BE|^2 = |EF|^2 + |BF|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + |BF|^2$ We also know that $|GA| = \frac{\cos{\alpha}}{2}$ so $|BG| = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2}$. From miter vector calculation and intercept theorem we know that $|BF| = \frac{|BG|}{\sin{\alpha}} = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}$ So we know the length of the vector $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + \left(\frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2{\alpha} + 1^2 - 2\cos{\alpha} + \cos^2{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} = \frac{2 - 2 \cos{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} $$ Using trigonometric identity $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{1 - \cos^2{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{(1 - \cos{\alpha})(1 + \cos{\alpha})} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}}$$ So if we would like to check condition $|BE| > limit$ we could use the following equation: $$ \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}} > {limit}^2$$ So: $$ \frac{1}{2 \cdot {limit}^2} - 1 > \cos{\alpha} $$ It means that if $\cos\alpha$ is below a given threshold, we should use bevel join. ## Too long vector in bevel join # As I showed above, this PR is fixing the bevel join by fixing the position of inner edge of the bevel join. You may see an example in this image (see the longest yellow vector): ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/58e13821-a1b6-4dd0-a49b-ade97edf9e73) The schematic of generating long edge: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/329b2b61-3c9d-4050-9623-7053c1f46248) As users may use shapes with different length edges, we cannot use a hard-coded limit for the length of the offset vector that creates the problems pointed on above image. However, when calculating the normal vectors, we need to calculate the edge width, so we may save them for later usage and limit the length of such problematic offset vector to length of shortest adjusted edge. In the Bevel limit section, we have an explicit formula for miter offset length. However, it contains a square root. And calculation of a square root is expensive on current CPU, so we would like to avoid it. However, the described problem happens only when $\measuredangle BAC$ is close to $180\degree$, but in such a situation, the $|\vec{BA} + \vec{AC}|$ is close to 1 (as resulted vector is close to diameter). So we could approximate the length of vector $\vec{BC}$ as 1. Then the estimated length of vector $\vec{BE}$ is $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ So if $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ is bigger than the length of half of any adjusted edges, we could calculate a new scaling factor with value $$\pm 0.5 \cdot\min(vec1\textunderscore{}len, vec2\textunderscore{}len))$$ # Additional notes ## Artifacts There are still artifacts in rendering ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/416df018-f293-425c-bd38-916ba8137934) --------- Co-authored-by: pre-commit-ci[bot] <66853113+pre-commit-ci[bot]@users.noreply.github.com> Co-authored-by: Carol Willing Co-authored-by: Juan Nunez-Iglesias ","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/commits/b2edccd6e40e04467ccfeec0257c2160783f7187"}]}

notes"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/milestones/46","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/milestone/46","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/milestones/46/labels","id":11616555,"node_id":"MI_kwDOCJ0aI84AsUEr","number":46,"title":"0.5.5","description":"","creator":{"login":"jni","id":492549,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5MjU0OQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/492549?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni","html_url":"https://github.com/jni","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"open_issues":1,"closed_issues":61,"state":"open","created_at":"2024-09-22T01:24:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:06Z","due_on":"2024-11-25T08:00:00Z","closed_at":null},"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-09-13T16:21:13Z","updated_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:17Z","closed_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:05Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/pulls/7268","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268","diff_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268.patch","merged_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:05Z"},"body":"# References and relevant issues # Description Similar to #7256 this PR is a speedup of face triangulation by avoiding a lot of reallocation of arrays. After long investigation, I found that using best way to speed this up is by avoiding the allocation of additional arrays by using numba. For shapes that do not have bevels: On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6775d94-a88b-4a99-959f-f4b6f7f768aa) In this PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a3a302c-2eb1-4ecd-8c38-a07617512ede) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d7f00a8-4d55-4bb2-9f49-955bc746e01f) As you may see, the time of `triangulate_edge` is reduced from 44s to 17s. Half of it is numba compilation. With sharp edges that require bevel join, the speedup is even bigger: Main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2a55f13-6ba7-4f1d-9ff9-56fa46fdf49c) PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6279350e-1e50-441e-a538-4f01b5420d40) PR with cache: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3c992928-b63c-42d7-be6d-d0ec7ec620f5) This PR also fixes the bevel join On main: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a24bc31-9777-4d60-81d5-ff6cc1b0d1a2) With PR: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b167a4d5-9bce-442c-80c6-d5a0c90e5f8d) In this PR I have added `examples/dev/triangle_edge.py` with a set of helper functions to visualize triangulation. These are helpful for debugging of triangulation problems. # Algorithm explanation Here I add some explanation to the code implementation of this PR. ## Calculation of miter vector # This is a screenshot of miter join: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08d2dd78-bb02-4da8-baa5-7b0596e1c3c7) And let's assume markings as on image: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0cec9ff-3786-4edc-805a-d45e58f33aab) The $\vec{BA}$ (blue) is half of the normal vector from vertex $n$ to $n+1$. The $\vec{AC}$ is minus half of the normal vector from vertex $n-1$ to $n$. The vector $\vec{AD}$ (green) is orthogonal to line $BA$ of length $0.5$ Based on these vectors, we could easily calculate $\vec{BC} = \vec{BA} + \vec{AC}$. However, the length of $|GC|$ may be different from 1. We would like to get the vector $\vec{BE}$ We will use here trigonometry and [Intercept theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercept_theorem) We know that $\frac{|GC|}{|AC|} = \sin{\alpha}$ and $|AC| = \frac{1}{2}$ so $|GC| = \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{2}$ From intercept theorem, we know that $\frac{|BC|}{|GC|} = \frac{|BE|}{|FE|}$. The length of $|FE| = \frac{1}{2}$. So $|BE| = |BC| \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$. That mens that $\vec{BE} = \vec{BC} \times \frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ ## Bevel limit # In general, we use miter join, however, if the angle is sharp, it may result in strange artifacts (see #6706). So if the join will be too long (The length of $\vec{BA}$ vector from above), we would like to change to a bevel join. So we need to calculate the length of $\vec{BE}$. We would like to avoid calculation of square root in every turn of the loop. From Pythagoras theorem we know that $|BE|^2 = |EF|^2 + |BF|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + |BF|^2$ We also know that $|GA| = \frac{\cos{\alpha}}{2}$ so $|BG| = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2}$. From miter vector calculation and intercept theorem we know that $|BF| = \frac{|BG|}{\sin{\alpha}} = \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}$ So we know the length of the vector $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2}^2 + \left(\frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{2\sin{\alpha}}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2{\alpha} + 1^2 - 2\cos{\alpha} + \cos^2{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} = \frac{2 - 2 \cos{\alpha}}{4\sin^2{\alpha}} $$ Using trigonometric identity $$|BE|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{1 - \cos^2{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos{\alpha}}{(1 - \cos{\alpha})(1 + \cos{\alpha})} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}}$$ So if we would like to check condition $|BE| > limit$ we could use the following equation: $$ \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos{\alpha}} > {limit}^2$$ So: $$ \frac{1}{2 \cdot {limit}^2} - 1 > \cos{\alpha} $$ It means that if $\cos\alpha$ is below a given threshold, we should use bevel join. ## Too long vector in bevel join # As I showed above, this PR is fixing the bevel join by fixing the position of inner edge of the bevel join. You may see an example in this image (see the longest yellow vector): ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/58e13821-a1b6-4dd0-a49b-ade97edf9e73) The schematic of generating long edge: ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/329b2b61-3c9d-4050-9623-7053c1f46248) As users may use shapes with different length edges, we cannot use a hard-coded limit for the length of the offset vector that creates the problems pointed on above image. However, when calculating the normal vectors, we need to calculate the edge width, so we may save them for later usage and limit the length of such problematic offset vector to length of shortest adjusted edge. In the Bevel limit section, we have an explicit formula for miter offset length. However, it contains a square root. And calculation of a square root is expensive on current CPU, so we would like to avoid it. However, the described problem happens only when $\measuredangle BAC$ is close to $180\degree$, but in such a situation, the $|\vec{BA} + \vec{AC}|$ is close to 1 (as resulted vector is close to diameter). So we could approximate the length of vector $\vec{BC}$ as 1. Then the estimated length of vector $\vec{BE}$ is $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ So if $\frac{1}{\sin{\alpha}}$ is bigger than the length of half of any adjusted edges, we could calculate a new scaling factor with value $$\pm 0.5 \cdot\min(vec1\textunderscore{}len, vec2\textunderscore{}len))$$ # Additional notes ## Artifacts There are still artifacts in rendering ![obraz](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/416df018-f293-425c-bd38-916ba8137934) ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/7268/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/7268/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/comments/2533395360","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/7268#issuecomment-2533395360","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/7268","id":2533395360,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCJ0aI86XAIug","user":{"login":"jni","id":492549,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5MjU0OQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/492549?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni","html_url":"https://github.com/jni","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:15Z","updated_at":"2024-12-11T01:17:15Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"🥳 🚀 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/comments/2533395360/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

🐛 Bug Report

Mousing over the canvas results in exceptions when 3D rendering RGB(A) data.

Most reproducible is for `attenuated_mip` but various exceptions may occur with other rendering modes:
```
File ~/src/napari/napari/layers/image/image.py:738, in Image._calculate_value_from_ray(self=<Image layer 'kidney'>, values=array([[264, 126, 181],

... 90, 118],
       [ 77,  90, 118]], dtype=uint16))

    735         * len(values_attenuated)
    736     )
    737     scale = np.exp(-self.attenuation * (sumval - 1))
--> 738     return values[np.nanargmin(values_attenuated * scale)]
        values_attenuated = array([[0.06203, 0.02833, 0.04176],
       [0.06203, 0.02833, 0.04176],
       ...,

       [264, 126, 181],
       ...,
       [ 77,  90, 118],

        np.nanargmin = <function nanargmin at 0x10604baf0>
    740 raise RuntimeError(  # pragma: no cover
    741     f'ray value calculation not implemented for {self.rendering}'
    742 )

ValueError: operands could not be broadcast together with shapes (32,3) (96,) 
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

Run this script, then mouse over the canvas:

```python
from skimage import data

import napari

viewer = napari.Viewer(ndisplay=3)

kidney = data.kidney()

viewer.add_image(kidney, rgb=True, rendering='attenuated_mip')

napari.run()
```

### 💡 Expected Behavior

No exceptions

This is due to the `Image._calculate_value_from_ray` assuming the data is single-channel. 🧰 Task

This is a meta issue for various Shapes selection & highlight issues:

- With a Shape selected, highlight-on-hover is glitchy: sometimes doesn’t fire, sometimes (in Vertex selection mode) removes the selection highlight leaving just vertexes
- With a Shape selected, Shift-Click doesn’t visually select a 2nd Shape, even though it will be selected — unlike the drag selection (which also has buggy highlight of the shapes)
For these two see:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b9f6bb38-ac04-4f71-a1f4-f9594a26b67f

Note: both of the above work correctly in 0.4.19post1, but not on main (with exception of the difference between shift-click multi selection and drag multi selection)

- https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7073 clicking on a Shape in Pan_Zoom mode highlights Shape


Minor:

1. [ ] diverging colormaps, of which we only offer PiYG. Looking at the [matplotlib diverging colormaps](https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/colors/colormaps.html#diverging), I think we should also offer RdBu at a minimum, maybe also BrBG and Spectral.

3. [ ] for 3D rendering, we need two new projection modes: mavip and attenuated_mavip, where we return the highest *absolute* value position, then color according to the signed value.

🐛 Bug

I found another issue with visualisation of highly packed points (see #2101). If coordinate differences are small (e.g: a cloud of points between 0 and 1), napari often messes up the foreground/background ordering of the points.

## To Reproduce
```python
v = napari.Viewer(ndisplay=3)
p = np.random.rand(100, 3) * (0.1, 0.01, 0.1)
v.add_points(p, size=0.01)
```
Then move the view around. Chances are, things are looking trippy!

## Expected behavior

Points are covering other points based on their distance from the camera.

## Environment

```yaml
napari: 0.4.3
Platform: Linux-5.10.7-arch1-1-x86_64-with-glibc2.32
Python: 3.9.1 (default, Dec 13 2020, 11:55:53) [GCC 10.2.0]
Qt: 5.15.2
PyQt5: 5.15.2
NumPy: 1.19.5
SciPy: 1.6.0
Dask: 2020.12.0
VisPy: 0.6.6

OpenGL:
- GL version: 4.6.0 NVIDIA 460.32.03
- MAX_TEXTURE_SIZE: 16384

Screens:
- screen #1: resolution 1920x1080, scale 1.0
- screen #2: resolution 1920x1200, scale 1.0

Plugins:
- mrcfile-reader: 0.1.2
- napari_plugin_engine: 0.1.9
- ndtiffs: 0.1.1
- svg: 0.1.4
```

I pared down [this example](https://vispy.org/gallery/scene/clipping_planes.html#sphx-glr-gallery-scene-clipping-planes-py) to:

```python
import numpy as np
from vispy import app, scene

# make the data
np.random.seed(1)
points = np.random.rand(100, 3) * (0.1, 0.01, 0.1)

# Prepare canvas
canvas = scene.SceneCanvas(
        keys='interactive', size=(800, 600), show=True
        )

# Set up a viewbox to display the image with interactive pan/zoom
view = canvas.central_widget.add_view()

# Add the markers
markers = scene.visuals.Markers(
        pos=points, size=100, parent=view.scene
        )

# set the camera
view.camera = scene.cameras.ArcballCamera(
        parent=view.scene,
        # play with the fov/perspective:
        #  - 0: reproduce the effect
        #  - 0.1: flickering/unstable
        #  - 45: things look great
        fov=0,
        # need the scale factor for the points to be
        # bunched up on macOS at least
        scale_factor=0.35,
        name='Arcball',
        )

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # run the app
    app.run()
```

As noted in the comment in the above snippet, setting a perspective projection makes the problem go away, so depending on what you're trying to do @cmalinmayor that might get you going. Some things don't work in perspective projection but point picking does since 0.5.2. As a reminder, you can set perspective by right-click dragging, or by right clicking on the 3D view button on the bottom left of the viewer.

As to fixing the issue, we'll have to make a corresponding issue on VisPy. Here are the relevant [vertex](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/blob/85c4bf944d4f38acc67c215548d3701adb8d9772/vispy/visuals/markers.py#L16-L87) and [fragment](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/blob/85c4bf944d4f38acc67c215548d3701adb8d9772/vispy/visuals/markers.py#L90-L210) shaders, though I don't really know what I'm doing reading them. 😅 Things that stand out to me:

- [`big_float` "prevents numerical imprecision"](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/blob/85c4bf944d4f38acc67c215548d3701adb8d9772/vispy/visuals/markers.py#L36) Does it? Does it really?? 😜 
- [`depth_change`](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/blob/85c4bf944d4f38acc67c215548d3701adb8d9772/vispy/visuals/markers.py#L171) has entirely too much arithmetic going on for my tastes. Also the TODO immediately above it 😂 
- [`gl_FragDepth =`](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/blob/85c4bf944d4f38acc67c215548d3701adb8d9772/vispy/visuals/markers.py#L208) — but I don't understand where gl_FragCoord.z is set — default OpenGL stuff?

Some help from our resident VisPy experts @brisvag and @aganders3 would be amazing. 🙏","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/comments/2538137612/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

🐛 Bug

I found another issue with visualisation of highly packed points (see #2101). If coordinate differences are small (e.g: a cloud of points between 0 and 1), napari often messes up the foreground/background ordering of the points.

## To Reproduce
```python
v = napari.Viewer(ndisplay=3)
p = np.random.rand(100, 3) * (0.1, 0.01, 0.1)
v.add_points(p, size=0.01)
```
Then move the view around. Chances are, things are looking trippy!

## Expected behavior

Points are covering other points based on their distance from the camera.

## Environment

```yaml
napari: 0.4.3
Platform: Linux-5.10.7-arch1-1-x86_64-with-glibc2.32
Python: 3.9.1 (default, Dec 13 2020, 11:55:53) [GCC 10.2.0]
Qt: 5.15.2
PyQt5: 5.15.2
NumPy: 1.19.5
SciPy: 1.6.0
Dask: 2020.12.0
VisPy: 0.6.6

OpenGL:
- GL version: 4.6.0 NVIDIA 460.32.03
- MAX_TEXTURE_SIZE: 16384

Screens:
- screen #1: resolution 1920x1080, scale 1.0
- screen #2: resolution 1920x1200, scale 1.0

Plugins:
- mrcfile-reader: 0.1.2
- napari_plugin_engine: 0.1.9
- ndtiffs: 0.1.1
- svg: 0.1.4
```

Description
Segmentation annotation often has a closed set of predefined classes such as `{1: 'person', 2: 'car', 3: 'road', 4: 'sky', 5: 'tree', ...}`, sometimes with tens of classes for one task. They can also be quite sparse like `[1, 10, 27, 255]` (e.g., 255 is often used as an ignore class).

The Labels layer currently does not provide a good way to show and navigate through labels as switching labels either by linear decreasing or increasing a label value or by typing it manually is completely not suitable in such cases, which may reduce productivity and may lead to errors and confusion while annotating. 

This PR introduces a new way of displaying and selecting labels: 
* It adds a new parameter `predefined_labels` to the Labels layer specifying a closed set of labels that allowed to be used in the layer, it can also provide a name for each label.
* When it is provided, instead of a spin box a new `QtLabelsCombobox` widget is displayed that shows a dropdown list of specified labels with its colors and names.
* It also changes behavior of forward and backward navigation through labels using keybindings. If `predefined_labels` is provided, `decrease_label_id`/`increase_label_id` methods select the closest label that is less or greater than the current selected label. They also do not allow going above or below the minimum or maximum label value.
* If names are specified, it augments the status message with the current label name in the same way as properties/features.


https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/1ae92409-7c24-40cf-a01f-55d95285d239

https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/ce266c11-3f75-454d-bb17-8f59cb90a762

I've added a usage example: `examples\add_predefined_labels_with_names.py`:

![image](https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/449ac958-37dc-4e29-a435-814319291600)


## Type of change
- [X] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)

# How has this been tested?
- [X] a test for the new QtLabelsCombobox is added
- [X] tests covering predefined_labels in the Labels layer are added
- [X] all tests pass with my change

## Final checklist:

- [ ] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [X] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective or that my feature works

#7268 optimised edge triangulation using numba. However, because numba is a JIT compiler, this means that the very first time we draw edges, the application becomes unresponsive as numba compiles. This is not a problem in later runs because we turn on numba caching.

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

1. launch napari in a new environment
2. create a shapes layer
3. start drawing

### 💡 Expected Behavior

drawing should be instantly responsive.

This can potentially be done by adding a compilation step at install time by pretending to draw.

Initially reported by @will-moore [on image.sc](). Running anything that creates a napari Viewer without launching a QApplication causes a QThread warning and Abort trap, including a nasty "crashed application" report on macOS.

```
$ python napari-no-run.py
WARNING: QThread: Destroyed while thread is still running
[1]    46560 abort      python napari-no-run.py
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

Run the following outside of an IPython/Jupyter context:

```python
import napari
viewer = napari.Viewer()
```

### 💡 Expected Behavior

Probably the QThread shouldn't be started, or we should find a way to close it cleanly when the application isn't running? CC @czak

### 🌎 Environment

When switching to 3D view in async, the viewer displays the already-loaded 2D array in 3D space before replacing the data with a real 3D node, asynchronously. Before the full 3D texture is loaded, and since #7126, hovering the mouse over the canvas triggers get_value, which gets evaluated to _get_value_3d, which fails because the array is 2D at this moment.


**Edit:** link to the traceback 😅: https://gist.github.com/jni/a244d3931f8eadd8fc7e64887feff2ca

Summary:

```pytb
File ~/projects/napari/napari/components/viewer_model.py:571, in ViewerModel._calc_status_from_cursor
--> 571     status = active.get_status(
File ~/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py:2142, in Layer.get_status
-> 2142     value = self.get_value(
File ~/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py:1404, in Layer.get_value
-> 1404         value = self._get_value_3d(
File ~/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py:687, in ScalarFieldBase._get_value_3d
--> 687     return self._get_value_ray(
File ~/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py:655, in ScalarFieldBase._get_value_ray
--> 655     values = im_slice[tuple(clamped.T)]
IndexError: too many indices for array: array is 2-dimensional, but 3 were indexed
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

1. run `NAPARI_ASYNC=1 napari https://uk1s3.embassy.ebi.ac.uk/idr/zarr/v0.4/idr0001A/2551.zarr/A/1/0`
2. select a visible image layer
3. switch to 3D mode
4. hover the mouse over the canvas

### 💡 Expected Behavior

No error should be raised.

#7268 optimised edge triangulation using numba. However, because numba is a JIT compiler, this means that the very first time we draw edges, the application becomes unresponsive as numba compiles. This is not a problem in later runs because we turn on numba caching.

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

1. launch napari in a new environment
2. create a shapes layer
3. start drawing

### 💡 Expected Behavior

drawing should be instantly responsive.

This can potentially be done by adding a compilation step at install time by pretending to draw.

Actually it would probably be a mess to implement — now functions that say "ok draw these shapes" would have to be "ok draw these shapes one way if we are in draw mode, another way if we are just drawing a big dataset". Since this only needs to happen once I really don't think that's the right path. But just for reference, all numba-decorated functions store the pure Python function in the `.py_func` attribute, in case anyone wants to attempt this surgery.

I don't know whether there is a public API for know whether a numba function has been compiled already or not.

Initially reported by @will-moore [on image.sc](https://forum.image.sc/t/napari-viewer-crashes-napari/106176). Running anything that creates a napari Viewer without launching a QApplication causes a QThread warning and Abort trap, including a nasty "crashed application" report on macOS.

```
$ python napari-no-run.py
WARNING: QThread: Destroyed while thread is still running
[1]    46560 abort      python napari-no-run.py
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

Run the following outside of an IPython/Jupyter context:

```python
import napari
viewer = napari.Viewer()
```

### 💡 Expected Behavior

Probably the QThread shouldn't be started, or we should find a way to close it cleanly when the application isn't running? CC @czak

stones/34","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/milestone/34","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/milestones/34/labels","id":7970323,"node_id":"MI_kwDOCJ0aI84AeZ4T","number":34,"title":"0.5","description":null,"creator":{"login":"jni","id":492549,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5MjU0OQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/492549?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni","html_url":"https://github.com/jni","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"open_issues":60,"closed_issues":35,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-05-11T23:50:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T04:22:19Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":14,"created_at":"2023-05-26T23:43:26Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T01:09:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/pulls/5875","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/5875","diff_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/5875.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/pull/5875.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"# Description
Segmentation annotation often has a closed set of predefined classes such as `{1: 'person', 2: 'car', 3: 'road', 4: 'sky', 5: 'tree', ...}`, sometimes with tens of classes for one task. They can also be quite sparse like `[1, 10, 27, 255]` (e.g., 255 is often used as an ignore class).

The Labels layer currently does not provide a good way to show and navigate through labels as switching labels either by linear decreasing or increasing a label value or by typing it manually is completely not suitable in such cases, which may reduce productivity and may lead to errors and confusion while annotating. 

This PR introduces a new way of displaying and selecting labels: 
* It adds a new parameter `predefined_labels` to the Labels layer specifying a closed set of labels that allowed to be used in the layer, it can also provide a name for each label.
* When it is provided, instead of a spin box a new `QtLabelsCombobox` widget is displayed that shows a dropdown list of specified labels with its colors and names.
* It also changes behavior of forward and backward navigation through labels using keybindings. If `predefined_labels` is provided, `decrease_label_id`/`increase_label_id` methods select the closest label that is less or greater than the current selected label. They also do not allow going above or below the minimum or maximum label value.
* If names are specified, it augments the status message with the current label name in the same way as properties/features.


https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/1ae92409-7c24-40cf-a01f-55d95285d239

https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/ce266c11-3f75-454d-bb17-8f59cb90a762

I've added a usage example: `examples\add_predefined_labels_with_names.py`:

![image](https://github.com/napari/napari/assets/5459767/449ac958-37dc-4e29-a435-814319291600)


## Type of change

feature or request"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-13T18:00:43Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T01:12:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## 🚀 Feature

Add the ability to 'Delete All Layers' from within the GUI. 

## Motivation

Presently, a user is able to delete selected layers with the traschan GUI icon (or `Del` key). However, if the GUI fills up with many layers, selecting all the layers can become cumbersome for users, especially for those with mobility challenges, trackpads, touchpads, or pens. I find that frequently the layer list can become quite long, especially if a a multi-dimensional image has a dimension read in to populate individual layers or I'm trying to evaluating parameters in an analysis and continually add layers. 

## Pitch

Add a special right-click context action to the trashcan icon to 'Delete All Layers'. Additionally, this could also contain a 'Delete all Unselected Layers' special context as well. Downside is the lack of intuitive access to the right-click actions right now.

## Alternatives

1. Add an additional icon next to the current Trashcan that serves as the 'Delete All Trashcan'. I imagine accidentally pressing this could be very frustrating. Adds bloat to UI.
2. Add this ability to the layers menu. This would not match the current use of napari. 
3. Add `shift-del` default keybinding to 'Delete All Layers' action.

Initially reported by @will-moore [on image.sc](https://forum.image.sc/t/napari-viewer-crashes-napari/106176). Running anything that creates a napari Viewer without launching a QApplication causes a QThread warning and Abort trap, including a nasty "crashed application" report on macOS.

```
$ python napari-no-run.py
WARNING: QThread: Destroyed while thread is still running
[1]    46560 abort      python napari-no-run.py
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

Run the following outside of an IPython/Jupyter context:

```python
import napari
viewer = napari.Viewer()
```

### 💡 Expected Behavior

Probably the QThread shouldn't be started, or we should find a way to close it cleanly when the application isn't running? CC @czak

### 🌎 Environment

```
napari: 0.5.5
Platform: macOS-15.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit
System: MacOS 15.1.1

request"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/milestones/34","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari/milestone/34","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/milestones/34/labels","id":7970323,"node_id":"MI_kwDOCJ0aI84AeZ4T","number":34,"title":"0.5","description":null,"creator":{"login":"jni","id":492549,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5MjU0OQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/492549?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni","html_url":"https://github.com/jni","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"open_issues":60,"closed_issues":35,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-05-11T23:50:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T04:22:19Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-08-02T04:23:44Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T07:29:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## 🚀 Feature

Currently we downscale when the data size exceeds the maximum OpenGL texture size along a dimension (typically, 16K or 32K in 2D, or 2K in 3D). The problem is that the user has no way to recover that detail. (See [related Zulip discussion](https://napari.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/212875-general/topic/Will.20napari.20automatically.20resample.20huge.20BMP.20images.20when.20open/near/455805501), which is just the most recent one among many.)

I think we originally decided for automatic downsample instead of automatic multiscale because in the absence of async rendering, multiscale was suuuuper slow and janky with remote data. Now it's only slightly slow and janky 😂 ([relevant image.sc post](https://forum.image.sc/t/napari-does-not-display-full-resolution/86982/31)), so imho we should auto-multiscale.

The relevant code path is here:

https://github.com/napari/napari/blob/e739cef6e5220e71ecbc293ee431a0819ba07b1a/napari/_vispy/layers/scalar_field.py#L109-L113

## Alternatives

We could opt to auto-multiscale *only* for NumPy arrays and/or *only* when async is turned on. I don't mind the only-for-numpy idea because everything should be super fast *and* it costs 0 RAM since the downsamples are just views.

For example, the tribolium embryo time series data is super janky to slice through in 3D if you use the full resolution, even with zarr on-disk. We need to figure out multiscale asynchronous load so that you could load and show the lowest res, then fire up a thread that loads the next higher res that fits in a texture, show that, etc.

On the other hand I think auto-multiscaling only numpy arrays might be a Good First Issue ™️.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/comments/2542935928/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The are not without names, but they are certainly in different Python frames: in one case, you launch the viewer in code, and in the other, you click a button within the first viewer and get a new viewer — the variable pointing to it is never going to be in the same namespace as the first viewer. As a totally inaccurate example, one would be "viewer" and the other would be somewhere like "viewer.window._qt_window._dock_widgets.getChildren()[0]". References and relevant issues
Closes: https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7412

# Description

As noted in the issue, Return, Enter, Tab already exit a LineEdit.
Meanwhile, the `clearFocus` was dropping focus on the dialog `OK` button and activating it, because by default when focus is placed using Return/Enter it will activate the button.
In this PR, only Capslock will trigger `clearFocus` because it doesn't do that, keeping the existing (albeit strange) behavior for that key.

References and relevant issues
Closes: https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7412

# Description

As noted in the issue, Return, Enter, Tab already exit a LineEdit.
Meanwhile, the `clearFocus` was dropping focus on the dialog `OK` button and activating it, because by default when focus is placed using Return/Enter it will activate the button.
In this PR, only Capslock will trigger `clearFocus` because it doesn't do that, keeping the existing (albeit strange) behavior for that key.

References and relevant issues
Closes: https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7412

# Description

As noted in the issue, Return, Enter, Tab already exit a LineEdit.
Meanwhile, the `clearFocus` was dropping focus on the dialog `OK` button and activating it, because by default when focus is placed using Return/Enter it will activate the button.
In this PR, only Capslock will trigger `clearFocus` because it doesn't do that, keeping the existing (albeit strange) behavior for that key.

expected"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-19T04:55:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T04:55:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### 🐛 Bug Report If your array object generates errors during slicing *and async slicing is on*, napari will simply hang and there will be no indication of what is going wrong: if you run the code below with `NAPARI_ASYNC=0`, then you will get the appropriate traceback: ```pytb $ NAPARI_ASYNC=0 python error-slicer.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/jni/projects/play/error-slicer.py", line 11, in viewer, layer = napari.imshow(arr) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/view_layers.py", line 405, in imshow return _make_viewer_then( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/view_layers.py", line 173, in _make_viewer_then added = method(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/utils/migrations.py", line 101, in _update_from_dict return func(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/components/viewer_model.py", line 1004, in add_image layer = Image(data, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 115, in __call__ obj._post_init() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py", line 311, in _post_init self.refresh() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1553, in refresh self._refresh_sync( File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1577, in _refresh_sync self.set_view_slice() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1269, in set_view_slice self._set_view_slice() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py", line 490, in _set_view_slice response = request() ^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 212, in __call__ else self._call_single_scale() ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 217, in _call_single_scale data = self._project_thick_slice(self.data, self.data_slice) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 316, in _project_thick_slice return np.asarray(data[slices]) ~~~~^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/play/error-slicer.py", line 7, in __getitem__ raise RuntimeError('knife block! 🥋') RuntimeError: knife block! 🥋 ``` ### 💡 Steps to Reproduce Run the code below with `NAPARI_ASYNC=1` or `NAPARI_ASYNC=0`. ```python import numpy as np import wrapt import napari class DontSliceMeBro(wrapt.ObjectProxy): def __getitem__(self, key): raise RuntimeError('knife block! 🥋') arr = DontSliceMeBro(np.array([[[1, 0.5], [0.3, 1]],] * 2)) viewer, layer = napari.imshow(arr) napari.run() ``` ### 💡 Expected Behavior There should be some indication that an error has occurred during slicing and that we are not actually doing anything. ### 🌎 Environment ``` napari: 0.5.5 Platform: macOS-15.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit System: MacOS 15.1.1 Python: 3.11.10 | packaged by conda-forge | Description

This PR implements a command palette widget.

![viewer-output](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40591297/212549098-17e42796-6114-4bb2-b699-3b61a61d32ea.gif)

Still work-in-progress, but here's the list of required functinalities and how much of them this PR has done (See #5430).

> Search logic: fuzzy matching ranked by match scoring

→ fuzzy matching is not implemented yet. Just match by `__contains__` for now.

> Command context: breadcrumbs for each search result (e.g., segment_blobs_and_things with_membranes [plugin name] > gaussian blur or preferences > plugin settings > plugin API) 

→ Contexts are auto-generated from the command ID (e.g. "napari:layer:XYZ" → "napari > layer > XYZ")

> Command coverage: napari menus & submenus, layer context menus, all plugin command/menu contributions as specified in the manifest

→ Currently, only those commands registered in the `app-model` way are available (so some commands such as "Open Files ..." are not available).

> Action effects: there would be an indication in the UI that shows whether it will run the command, or direct to a dialog and additional input required from user

→ Not yet, but current implementation is already prepared for it.

> Unavailable commands: gray out command options that aren’t available given the context. For example “split RGB” would be grayed out if the user hasn’t selected an image layer

→ The "when" parameters of commands are evaluated and unavaliable ones are grayed out.


## Type of change

- [ ] Bug-fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] This change requires a documentation update

# References


#5430

# How has this been tested?

- [ ] example: the test suite for my feature covers cases x, y, and z
- [ ] example: all tests pass with my change
- [ ] example: I check if my changes works with both PySide and PyQt backends
      as there are small differences between the two Qt bindings.  

## Final checklist:
- [ ] My PR is the minimum possible work for the desired functionality
- [ ] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [ ] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective or that my feature works
- [ ] If I included new strings, I have used `trans.` to make them localizable.
      For more information see our [translations guide](https://napari.org/developers/translations.html).

```pytb
  Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "/home/runner/work/napari/napari/napari/_qt/widgets/qt_command_palette.py", line 193, in data
      return QtCore.QVariant()
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  AttributeError: module 'qtpy.QtCore' has no attribute 'QVariant'
```

it turns out that PySide2 doesn't have QVariant at all. I found this SO post:

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56117996/qvariant-alternative-when-migrating-from-pyqt5-to-pyside2

which suggests that one should instead return None as the sentinel value. Testing locally that works with PyQt5. I've pushed that change which hopefully gets this PR green. 🤞 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/issues/comments/2552833611/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

🧰 Task

This is a meta issue for various Shapes selection & highlight issues:

- With a Shape selected, highlight-on-hover is glitchy: sometimes doesn’t fire, sometimes (in Vertex selection mode) removes the selection highlight leaving just vertexes
- With a Shape selected, Shift-Click doesn’t visually select a 2nd Shape, even though it will be selected — unlike the drag selection (which also has buggy highlight of the shapes)
For these two see:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b9f6bb38-ac04-4f71-a1f4-f9594a26b67f

Note: both of the above work correctly in 0.4.19post1, but not on main (with exception of the difference between shift-click multi selection and drag multi selection)

- https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7073 clicking on a Shape in Pan_Zoom mode highlights Shape

objects","user":{"login":"DragaDoncila","id":17995243,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTk1MjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17995243?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila","html_url":"https://github.com/DragaDoncila","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"# References and relevant issues
Addresses issue reported in this [zulip comment](https://napari.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/309872-plugins/topic/Reader.20plugin.20with.20connected.20event/near/485161161)

# Description
Extends `add_layers_with_plugins` to handle instantiated `Layer` objects returned from `reader` plugins, not just `LayerDataTuple`s. 

This allows plugin developers to instantiate layers and connect callbacks to them (if desired) before returning from the reader.

Note: this plugin sets the source on the returned `Layer` if and only if the plugin developer has left both `path` and `reader_plugin` of the Layer's source blank. We may want to **always** override the source of the layer, if we judge this is likely to lead to better UI. In order to set the source of an already created layer, I had to add a `source` setter, which is currently public. We may want to guard it, so that users/plugin developers can't override a `Layer'`s source and it's entirely within our control?

objects","user":{"login":"DragaDoncila","id":17995243,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTk1MjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17995243?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila","html_url":"https://github.com/DragaDoncila","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DragaDoncila/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"# References and relevant issues
Addresses issue reported in this [zulip comment](https://napari.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/309872-plugins/topic/Reader.20plugin.20with.20connected.20event/near/485161161)

# Description
Extends `add_layers_with_plugins` to handle instantiated `Layer` objects returned from `reader` plugins, not just `LayerDataTuple`s. 

This allows plugin developers to instantiate layers and connect callbacks to them (if desired) before returning from the reader.

Note: this plugin sets the source on the returned `Layer` if and only if the plugin developer has left both `path` and `reader_plugin` of the Layer's source blank. We may want to **always** override the source of the layer, if we judge this is likely to lead to better UI. In order to set the source of an already created layer, I had to add a `source` setter, which is currently public. We may want to guard it, so that users/plugin developers can't override a `Layer'`s source and it's entirely within our control?

References and relevant issues
Closes: https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7412

# Description

As noted in the issue, Return, Enter, Tab already exit a LineEdit.
Meanwhile, the `clearFocus` was dropping focus on the dialog `OK` button and activating it, because by default when focus is placed using Return/Enter it will activate the button.
In this PR, only Capslock will trigger `clearFocus` because it doesn't do that, keeping the existing (albeit strange) behavior for that key.

🐛 Bug Report If your array object generates errors during slicing *and async slicing is on*, napari will simply sit on the loading animation for that layer and there will be no indication of what is going wrong: if you run the code below with `NAPARI_ASYNC=0`, then you will get the appropriate traceback: ```pytb $ NAPARI_ASYNC=0 python error-slicer.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/jni/projects/play/error-slicer.py", line 11, in viewer, layer = napari.imshow(arr) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/view_layers.py", line 405, in imshow return _make_viewer_then( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/view_layers.py", line 173, in _make_viewer_then added = method(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/utils/migrations.py", line 101, in _update_from_dict return func(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/components/viewer_model.py", line 1004, in add_image layer = Image(data, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 115, in __call__ obj._post_init() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py", line 311, in _post_init self.refresh() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1553, in refresh self._refresh_sync( File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1577, in _refresh_sync self.set_view_slice() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/base/base.py", line 1269, in set_view_slice self._set_view_slice() File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/_scalar_field/scalar_field.py", line 490, in _set_view_slice response = request() ^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 212, in __call__ else self._call_single_scale() ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 217, in _call_single_scale data = self._project_thick_slice(self.data, self.data_slice) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/napari/napari/layers/image/_slice.py", line 316, in _project_thick_slice return np.asarray(data[slices]) ~~~~^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/jni/projects/play/error-slicer.py", line 7, in __getitem__ raise RuntimeError('knife block! 🥋') RuntimeError: knife block! 🥋 ``` ### 💡 Steps to Reproduce Run the code below with `NAPARI_ASYNC=1` or `NAPARI_ASYNC=0`. ```python import numpy as np import wrapt import napari class DontSliceMeBro(wrapt.ObjectProxy): def __getitem__(self, key): raise RuntimeError('knife block! 🥋') arr = DontSliceMeBro(np.array([[[1, 0.5], [0.3, 1]],] * 2)) viewer, layer = napari.imshow(arr) napari.run() ``` ### 💡 Expected Behavior There should be some indication that an error has occurred during slicing and that we are not actually doing anything. ### 🌎 Environment ``` napari: 0.5.5 Platform: macOS-15.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit Description

This PR implements a command palette widget.

![viewer-output](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40591297/212549098-17e42796-6114-4bb2-b699-3b61a61d32ea.gif)

Still work-in-progress, but here's the list of required functinalities and how much of them this PR has done (See #5430).

> Search logic: fuzzy matching ranked by match scoring

→ fuzzy matching is not implemented yet. Just match by `__contains__` for now.

> Command context: breadcrumbs for each search result (e.g., segment_blobs_and_things with_membranes [plugin name] > gaussian blur or preferences > plugin settings > plugin API) 

→ Contexts are auto-generated from the command ID (e.g. "napari:layer:XYZ" → "napari > layer > XYZ")

> Command coverage: napari menus & submenus, layer context menus, all plugin command/menu contributions as specified in the manifest

→ Currently, only those commands registered in the `app-model` way are available (so some commands such as "Open Files ..." are not available).

> Action effects: there would be an indication in the UI that shows whether it will run the command, or direct to a dialog and additional input required from user

→ Not yet, but current implementation is already prepared for it.

> Unavailable commands: gray out command options that aren’t available given the context. For example “split RGB” would be grayed out if the user hasn’t selected an image layer

→ The "when" parameters of commands are evaluated and unavaliable ones are grayed out.


## Type of change

- [ ] Bug-fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] This change requires a documentation update

# References


#5430

# How has this been tested?

- [ ] example: the test suite for my feature covers cases x, y, and z
- [ ] example: all tests pass with my change
- [ ] example: I check if my changes works with both PySide and PyQt backends
      as there are small differences between the two Qt bindings.  

## Final checklist:
- [ ] My PR is the minimum possible work for the desired functionality
- [ ] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [ ] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective or that my feature works
- [ ] If I included new strings, I have used `trans.` to make them localizable.
      For more information see our [translations guide](https://napari.org/developers/translations.html).

Initially reported by @will-moore [on image.sc](https://forum.image.sc/t/napari-viewer-crashes-napari/106176). Running anything that creates a napari Viewer without launching a QApplication causes a QThread warning and Abort trap, including a nasty "crashed application" report on macOS.

```
$ python napari-no-run.py
WARNING: QThread: Destroyed while thread is still running
[1]    46560 abort      python napari-no-run.py
```

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

Run the following outside of an IPython/Jupyter context:

```python
import napari
viewer = napari.Viewer()
```

### 💡 Expected Behavior

Probably the QThread shouldn't be started, or we should find a way to close it cleanly when the application isn't running? CC @czak

### 🌎 Environment

```
napari: 0.5.5
Platform: macOS-15.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit
System: MacOS 15.1.1
Python: 3.11.10 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Sep 30 2024, 17:58:42) [Clang 17.0.6 ]
Qt: 5.15.8
PyQt5: 5.15.9
NumPy: 1.26.4

psygnal: 0.11.1
npe2: 0.7.7
pydantic: 2.9.2

OpenGL:
  - GL version:  2.1 Metal - 89.3
  - MAX_TEXTURE_SIZE: 16384
  - GL_MAX_3D_TEXTURE_SIZE: 2048

Screens:
  - screen 1: resolution 1800x1169, scale 2.0

Optional:
  - numba: 0.60.0
  - triangle not installed
  - napari-plugin-manager: 0.1.3

Settings path:
  - /Users/jni/Library/Application Support/napari/all_f206df4881e2999baa22e6df448393db299c70d6/settings.yaml
Plugins:
  - affinder: 0.4.0 (10 contributions)

These failures were caused by an intervening frame in QtPy when using the Qt6 backend, because QtPy replaced some removed functions in the Qt5->Qt6 transition with their own implementations:

https://github.com/spyder-ide/qtpy/blob/1d2a1eae43bda2a3d0efef605cafc5462389ec03/qtpy/QtWidgets.py#L69-L105

Therefore, when looking for "first frame in the call stack that is not in napari", the frame capture code would stop in QtPy. By adding QtPy to the list of ignored prefixes, this PR fixes the issue in the Qt6 case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari-console/issues/43/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari-console/issues/43/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari-console/issues/comments/2557033504","html_url":"https://github.com/napari/napari-console/pull/43#issuecomment-2557033504","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari-console/issues/43","id":2557033504,"node_id":"IC_kwDOE5DDec6YaTwg","user":{"login":"jni","id":492549,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5MjU0OQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/492549?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni","html_url":"https://github.com/jni","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jni/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-20T13:36:32Z","updated_at":"2024-12-20T13:36:32Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Brilliant. {"repository_id":328254329,"push_id":21807722768,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"fe3dcf084f3771e520717c00fb877424718e85c1","before":"2e9ca54936d33a5282107c3167252aac23c1233f","commits":[{"sha":"fe3dcf084f3771e520717c00fb877424718e85c1","author":{"email":"b9319e2414749837dadc4d179d8c41f306ae7b76@users.noreply.github.com","name":"Daniel Althviz Moré"},"message":"Fix frame capture when using PyQt6 (#43)

See
https://github.com/napari/napari-console/issues/40#issuecomment-2533216712,
https://github.com/napari/napari-console/pull/42#issuecomment-2542733251

These failures were caused by an intervening frame in QtPy when using
the Qt6 backend, because QtPy replaced some removed functions in the
Qt5->Qt6 transition with their own implementations:


https://github.com/spyder-ide/qtpy/blob/1d2a1eae43bda2a3d0efef605cafc5462389ec03/qtpy/QtWidgets.py#L69-L105

Therefore, when looking for "first frame in the call stack that is not
in napari", the frame capture code would stop in QtPy. By adding QtPy to
the list of ignored prefixes, this PR fixes the issue in the Qt6 case.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari-console/commits/fe3dcf084f3771e520717c00fb877424718e85c1"}]}

{"repository_id":144513571,"push_id":21825192841,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"a5d6af0eb0e60dbaeb7f22ac4da7cba11ad9feb6","before":"c1a9153aab79d712041381f64709d7edc908f405","commits":[{"sha":"a5d6af0eb0e60dbaeb7f22ac4da7cba11ad9feb6","author":{"email":"f2e539f865a022dd4c686415db2ca1deb56cad41@gmail.com","name":"Grzegorz Bokota"},"message":"Elide layer name in the middle instead of the end (#7461)

# References and relevant issues

closes #7460

# Description

Layer names can often be long, and the distinguishing characteristic is
as often at the end of the layer name as at the start. When a layer name
is too long to display, we should therefore put the ellipsis in the
middle of the layer name, rather than at the end.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/commits/a5d6af0eb0e60dbaeb7f22ac4da7cba11ad9feb6"}]}

🐛 Bug Report

I have recently observed a change of behavior after updating to 0.5.5 (from ~0.4.17 I think) with the shapes layer :  I plot shapes on a Z stack and I want different shapes on each slice of the stack, just like in this example : https://napari.org/dev/gallery/nD_shapes.html
Before, on each slice the shape corresponding to the slice was appearing and could be edited, as wanted. with the new release, the shape in each slice also appears but it is not possible to select and edit it because all the other shapes from all the other slices also appear when I move the mouse over. The problem is visible in the n_Shapes example. Has the way shapes are handled changed? many thanks again

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

with this example from napari website:
https://napari.org/dev/gallery/nD_shapes.html

🐛 Bug Report

I have recently observed a change of behavior after updating to 0.5.5 (from ~0.4.17 I think) with the shapes layer :  I plot shapes on a Z stack and I want different shapes on each slice of the stack, just like in this example : https://napari.org/dev/gallery/nD_shapes.html
Before, on each slice the shape corresponding to the slice was appearing and could be edited, as wanted. with the new release, the shape in each slice also appears but it is not possible to select and edit it because all the other shapes from all the other slices also appear when I move the mouse over. The problem is visible in the n_Shapes example. Has the way shapes are handled changed? many thanks again

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

with this example from napari website:
https://napari.org/dev/gallery/nD_shapes.html

shapes from all the z-slices are selectable in any slice, without being visible

### 💡 Expected Behavior

on previous version (~0.4.17) only the shape corresponding to the slice was visible/selectable/editable 

### 🌎 Environment

napari 0.5.5 python 3.10 windows10

### 💡 Additional Context

- With a Shape selected, highlight-on-hover is glitchy: sometimes doesn’t fire, sometimes (in Vertex selection mode) removes the selection highlight leaving just vertexes
- With a Shape selected, Shift-Click doesn’t visually select a 2nd Shape, even though it will be selected — unlike the drag selection (which also has buggy highlight of the shapes)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b9f6bb38-ac04-4f71-a1f4-f9594a26b67f

Note: both of the above work correctly in 0.4.19post1, but not on main (with exception of the difference between shift-click multi selection and drag multi selection)

- https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7073 clicking on a Shape in Pan_Zoom mode highlights Shape

{"repository_id":144513571,"push_id":21825460964,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"b9e9e4a63a69c2ee7a71f46a55e3646c8e3f081a","before":"282aae62d77a473dcea554a1e18188f64c04f2d3","commits":[{"sha":"b9e9e4a63a69c2ee7a71f46a55e3646c8e3f081a","author":{"email":"79fc9818fc99d86802995c60f43011ffc0efb22a@users.noreply.github.com","name":"Peter Sobolewski"},"message":"[Bugfix] Don't exit Preferences widget when using Return/Enter to confirm a shortcut (#7420)

# References and relevant issues
Closes: https://github.com/napari/napari/issues/7412

# Description

As noted in the issue, Return, Enter, Tab already exit a LineEdit.
Meanwhile, the `clearFocus` was dropping focus on the dialog `OK` button
and activating it, because by default when focus is placed using
Return/Enter it will activate the button.
In this PR, only Capslock will trigger `clearFocus` because it doesn't
do that, keeping the existing (albeit strange) behavior for that key.

---------

Co-authored-by: Daniel Althviz Moré <16781833+dalthviz@users.noreply.github.com>","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/napari/napari/commits/b9e9e4a63a69c2ee7a71f46a55e3646c8e3f081a"}]}

🐛 Bug Report

If you use the GUI Preferences > Shortcuts editor, you can unbind a keybinding by double-clicking it and then deleting the existing keybind. But then to confirm that you need to press enter, which closes the window, presumably because it procs the OK. This is a bit unexpected, because you don't know what happened and quite annoying if you want to edit multiple keybinds.

Likewise, it seems impossible to bind Enter/Return to anything, because the window closes.

### 💡 Steps to Reproduce

1. Open the Preferences > Shortcuts.
2a. Double-click any binding and press Delete to unbind and then Enter/Return to confirm.
2b. Double-click any binding and try to set it to Enter/Return.

### 💡 Expected Behavior

When in Edit mode of a keybind, Enter shouldn't close the window.

Workaround:
For the first case (the unbinding) one can confirm it by clicking into a different keybind.
I can't find a workaround for the Enter case.

So I had a look and made an in-passing improvement but would like to use this PR to make a few more. - [x] replace magic_factory use with magicgui — it's more appropriate here and makes the code easier to read. - [ ] should napari.run() be under an `if __name__ == '__main__'` clause? (@lucyleeow @melissawm I don't actually understand the gallery embedding process...) - [ ] the gallery screenshot shows an empty viewer even though the example calls the widget once. I think this is because the processing happens in a separate thread and hasn't had time to execute. Can we add some sort of wait/callback to make sure that the functions have updated at least once? - [ ] I think we should add at least one more interesting wave so you can immediately see the grid and layer interactions (see example screenshot below). It makes the code a bit more complicated but the image *way* more interesting... - [ ] I'm thinking of a new gallery tag — "teaching" "learning" "teaching_and_learning", ...? Any suggestions? (The triangulation example I would move from dev and use that tag, too.)
